perturbation.original_text: 1.1 shall keep secret and confidential our discussions and any information about the Business supplied to you or your advisers by us or by our advisers, whether before or after this Agreement and any analyses or other documents which may be made or prepared by you which contai n or otherwise reflect such information or your review of or interest in the Business (“Confidential Information”), and not disclose to any third party any Co nfidential Information (except such information which comes into the public domain other than as a result of the disclosure by you or by your employees, agents or advisers who need to know such information for the purpose of eval uating whether or not, and on what terms, you might proceed with the proposed purchase);
perturbation.changed_text: 1.1 shall keep secret and confidential our discussions and any information about the Business supplied to you or your advisers by us or by our advisers, whether before or after this Agreement and any analyses or other documents which may be made or prepared by you which contai n or otherwise reflect such information or your review of or interest in the Business (“Confidential Information”), and may disclose to any third party any Confidential Information at their sole discretion;
perturbation.explanation: This modification allows the Recipient to disclose Confidential Information to any third party at their sole discretion, contradicting the original intent of maintaining confidentiality. The initial agreement restricts disclosure except for information in the public domain. This conflicts with the core purpose of a confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification negates the protection of trade secrets by allowing disclosure, directly conflicting with laws designed to protect confidential business information.

Civil proceedings (a) The Attorney General may, in a civil action, obtain appropriate injunctive relief against any violation of this chapter. (b) Private Civil Actions.- (1) In general .-An owner of a trade secret that is misappropriated may bring a civil action under this subsection if the trade secret is related to a product or service used in, or intended for use in, interstate or foreign commerce. (2) Civil seizure.- (A) In
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the purpose of maintaining confidentiality, which is central to trade secret protection. The original text restricts disclosure, while the modified text allows for disclosure at the recipient's sole discretion. This fundamentally undermines the confidentiality obligation. 18 USC 1836 protects trade secrets, and the modification weakens this protection by allowing disclosure.

perturbation.original_text: 1.6 shall, immediately upon written request fr om us, or without such request if you cease to be interested in purchasing the Business, return to us all written Confidential Information and expunge an y Confidential Information from any computer, word processor or other simil ar device in your possession or under your custody or control, without k eeping any copies, extracts or other reproductions thereof, and either return to us or destroy (such destruction to be certified in writing by you or a senior employee of yours who has supervised such destruction) all notes and memoran da prepared by you, your employees, agents or advisers, and any copies thereo f relating to any of the Confidential Information or the negotiations concerning the proposed sale of the Business;
perturbation.changed_text: 1.6 shall, only upon written request from us and a court order, return to us all written Confidential Information; destroying any Confidential Information is purely optional.
perturbation.explanation: The modification changes the conditions for returning confidential information, now requiring a court order. The removal of mandatory destruction creates a conflict with standard confidentiality practices.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Digital Millennium Copyright Act (DMCA), 17 U.S. Code § 1201
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification weakens the control of confidential information potentially conflicting with provisions protecting intellectual property under the DMCA, as the recipient can indefinitely retain copies of digital confidential information.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract significantly weakens confidentiality protections, contradicting standard legal practice. The original text mandates the return of confidential information upon written request or cessation of purchase interest, along with the destruction of all copies. The changed text requires a court order for the return of information and makes the destruction of confidential information optional. This directly undermines the protection of confidential information, as the recipient can retain copies indefinitely, potentially conflicting with intellectual property protection principles. While the provided snippets do not directly address this scenario, the modification's shift from mandatory action to optional compliance establishes a legal contradiction, making the contract less enforceable and more susceptible to disputes regarding confidentiality.

perturbation.original_text: 1.7 shall not, and shall procure that any associated company of yours shall not, employ, seek to employ, or solicit any pe rson employed by us or the Business or who holds or otherwise has access to Confid ential Information either at the date of this Agreement or at any time during negotiations for the sale of the Business or during the period of twelve months from the date of this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 1.7 may, at any time, employ any person formerly employed by us or the Business.
perturbation.explanation: This alteration eliminates the non-solicitation clause, directly contradicting typical protections against poaching employees who have confidential information. This poses a risk of misappropriation.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Economic Espionage Act of 1996, 18 U.S. Code § 1831
perturbation.law_explanation: By allowing the recipient to employ individuals with access to confidential information, the modification increases the risk of economic espionage, potentially violating laws designed to prevent the theft of trade secrets.
perturbation.justification: The original text prevents soliciting employees, protecting confidential information. The changed text explicitly allows employing anyone formerly employed, thus eliminating that protection. The Law Explanation suggests this increases the risk of economic espionage, potentially violating laws designed to prevent the theft of trade secrets. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing the Economic Espionage Act of 1996 (18 U.S.C. 1 note), indicates federal concern with protecting trade secrets. The modified text directly undermines measures preventing confidential information misuse, creating a direct contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 2.5 This agreement shall expire two years from the date of execution. Any agreement created by acceptance of this Co nfidential Information shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of (Enter State) and you hereby submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of (Enter State) in relation thereto.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.5 This agreement shall have no expiration date. Any agreement created by acceptance of this Co nfidential Information shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of (Enter State); disputes are to be settled through arbitration.
perturbation.explanation: The removal of the expiration date turns the agreement into a potentially perpetual obligation. Changing the dispute resolution from court jurisdiction to arbitration removes legal oversight from a court of law.
perturbation.law_explanation: While the FAA generally supports arbitration, the modification removes the 'exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts', which, depending on the Enter State, might conflict with local laws regarding the scope and enforceability of arbitration agreements, particularly in relation to matters involving significant public interest.

(1/4/1995) Back to Original Document << Previous TITLE 9 / CHAPTER 1 / § 2 Next >> [Print] [Print selection] [OLRC Home] Help 9 USC 2 : Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate Text contains those laws in effect on July 12, 2025 From Title 9-ARBITRATION CHAPTER 1-GENERAL PROVISIONS Jump To: Source Credit Miscellaneous Amendments Effective Date §2. Validity, irrevocability, and enforcement of agreements to arbitrate A written provision in any maritime transaction or a contract evidencing a transaction involving commerce to settle by arbitration a controversy thereafter arising out of such contract or transaction, or the refusal to perform the whole or any part thereof, or an agreement in writing to submit to arbitration an existing controversy arising ou
perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies court jurisdiction for dispute resolution, while the changed text mandates arbitration. The Law Explanation suggests this shift could conflict with state laws regarding the enforceability of arbitration agreements, especially involving significant public interest matters. While 9 USC 2, provided in Scraped Law Snippet 2, generally supports arbitration agreements, the Law Explanation indicates that the removal of 'exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts' creates a potential contradiction in the context of state-specific arbitration laws. The original text ensured court oversight, which is now removed. This could lead to the application of arbitration even in cases where it is legally inappropriate according to state law.

perturbation.explanation: Changing 'Confidentiality Agreement' to 'Publicity Agreement' fundamentally alters the purpose of the document. This creates a contradiction between the document's title and its content, which still discusses restrictions on information disclosure. It implies that the agreement focuses on promoting information rather than protecting it.
perturbation.law_explanation: By misrepresenting the nature of the agreement, this change could potentially violate the Lanham Act, which prohibits false or misleading representations in commerce. The title now directly contradicts the substance of the agreement, which still seeks to restrict information.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: 15 U.S. Code § 1125 - False designations of origin, false descriptions, and dilution forbidden | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 15 U.S. Code § 1125 - False designations of origin, false descriptions, and dilution forbidden U.S. Code Notes Authorities (CFR) prev | next (a) Civil action (1) Any person who, on or in connection with any goods or services, or any container for goods, uses in commerce any word, term, name, symbol, or device, or any combination thereof, or any false designation of origin, false or misleading description of fact, or false or misleading representation of fact, which— (A) is likely to cause confusion, or to cause mistake, or to deceive as to the affiliation, connection, or association of such person with another person , or as to the origin, sponsorship, or approval of his or her goods, services, or commercial activities by another person , or (B) in commercial advertising or promotion, misrepresents the nature, characteristics, qualities, or geographic origin of his or her or another person ’s goods, services, or commercial activities, shall be liable in a civil action by any person who believes that he or she is or is likely to be damaged by such act. (2) As used in this subsection, the term “ any person ” includes any State, instrumentality of a State or employee of a State or instrumentality of a State acting in his or her official capacity. Any State, and any such instrumentality, officer, or employee, shall be subject to the provisions of this chapter in the same manner and to the same extent as any nongovernmental entity. (3) In a civil action for trade dress infringement under this chapter for trade dress not registered on the principal register, the person who asserts trade dress protection has the burden of proving that the matter sought to be protected is not functional. (b) Importation Any goods marked or labeled in contravention of th
perturbation.justification: The change from 'Confidentiality Agreement' to 'Publicity Agreement' directly contradicts the core purpose of a confidentiality agreement, which is to protect sensitive information. The Lanham Act, as quoted in Scraped Law Snippet 2, prohibits 'false or misleading representation of fact' in commerce. By misrepresenting the document's purpose through its title, the modified text potentially violates this provision because the title now falsely suggests the agreement is about promoting information rather than restricting its disclosure, which can be misleading to involved parties.
